questions.imageAlt: Adobe Express interface showing a Recent uploads section with two image cards (a beach scene and a dog photo). Each card has a more actions button (three dots icon). The Stark Annotations sidebar displays Focus Order annotation with tab stop 5 labeled recent uploads and a note stating: User's uploaded content area is a single tab stop with arrow key operations. Focus order markers show: (1) back button next to Recent uploads header, (2) resize tool, (3) crop tool, (4) list view button, and (5) the content area containing the image cards.
questions.question: Based on these annotations, how would a keyboard user get to the more actions button on the card with a dog on it? Assume tab stop one is the back button next to recent uploads.

options.text: Tab 8 times
Tab 5 times, right arrow 3 times
Tab 6 times
Tab 5 times, right arrow once, tab 1 more time

feedback.a: Incorrect. This assumes each element receives its own tab stop, ignoring the arrow key navigation pattern.
feedback.b: Incorrect. Only one right arrow press is needed to move from the first card to the second.
feedback.c: Incorrect. Tab 6 would move past the content area entirely.
feedback.d: Correct. The annotation specifies that the content area is a single tab stop (tab stop 5) with arrow key operations.

questions.imageAlt: A dropdown menu showing two options: Duplicate with a duplicate icon, and Delete (in red text) with a red trash icon. Focus order annotations incorrectly show four separate tab stops: (1) on Duplicate text, (2) on the duplicate icon, (3) on Delete text, and (4) on the trash icon. An X button appears to close the menu. Below the menu are layer properties including Drop shadow, Stroke, and Color overlay options.
questions.question: A dropdown menu shows two options: "Duplicate" and "Delete." Focus order annotations show four separate tab stops on each text and icon. What feedback would you give?

options.text: The menu items are operated by arrow keys, so this is one tab stop
The Delete item uses color alone to indicate it's a negative action
The menu items receive one tab stop each, so there are only two tab stops, not four
The Blueline has been specified perfectly, no notes

feedback.a: Correct. Menu components follow the ARIA menu pattern where the entire menu receives a single tab stop with arrow key navigation.
feedback.b: Incorrect. The Delete item also has a distinct trash icon, so color is not the only indicator.
feedback.c: Incorrect. A menu should have ONE tab stop total for all items, not one per menu item.
feedback.d: Incorrect. The annotation shows four separate tab stops for two menu items, which violates standard menu keyboard patterns.

questions.imageAlt: A modal dialog titled Invite people to access, contribute, or review for sharing Hilton Sales Strategy. The modal contains an email input field with three email addresses added, a Reviewer dropdown, a Message text area with placeholder text, and two buttons at the bottom: Cancel and Send (blue). An X button appears in the top-right corner to close the modal. Below the modal, the background shows Share Hilton Sales Strategy with sharing details.
questions.question: What are three ways to close a modal dialog without creating any invitations?

options.text: Control + Alt + Delete
The X button, the Cancel button, and the Escape key
The X button, the Cancel button, and the End key
Command + Alt + Delete

feedback.a: Incorrect. This is a system-level keyboard shortcut, not related to closing modal dialogs.
feedback.b: Correct. These are the three standard methods for dismissing a modal without taking action.
feedback.c: Incorrect. The End key is not a standard method for closing modals. The Escape key is.
feedback.d: Incorrect. This is a system-level function, not a web application interaction.

questions.imageAlt: The Stark Contrast and Accessibility Checker panel showing Touch Targets results. The panel displays 12 x 12 as the target size in pixels for pointer inputs. Under AA requirements, both Width (24px) and Height (24px) show red X error icons. Under AAA requirements, both Width (44px) and Height (44px) also show red X error icons. The Recommendations section shows iOS: 44 x 44 pt and Material design: 48 x 48 dp. In the background, two checkbox options are visible: Reverse clip playback (checked, with purple highlight) and Maintain pitch (unchecked).
questions.question: A checkbox measures 12x12 pixels. Stark displays errors for AA and AAA conformance. How should you address this?

options.text: We have to make the box at least 24 by 24 pixels
We can defer the results as they mention AAA conformance
This is a false positive as the hit area is sufficient
We can make the box reach 24x24 when a user hovers over it

feedback.a: Incorrect. WCAG SC 2.5.8 requires the touch TARGET (interactive area) to be 24x24, not the visible graphic.
feedback.b: Incorrect. The tool shows failures for both AA AND AAA. SC 2.5.8 is a Level AA requirement.
feedback.c: Correct. Automated tools measure the visible element size, not the actual interactive hit area. The visual checkbox may be 12x12 but the clickable region extends beyond.
feedback.d: Incorrect. Touch targets must meet size requirements in their default state, not only on hover.

questions.imageAlt: A horizontal Tab Group component with three tabs: Usage (with a settings/sliders icon), User intent (with a target icon), and Credits (with a chart icon). The Usage tab appears selected with an underline indicator. Focus order annotations incorrectly show three separate tab stops: (1) on Usage, (2) on User intent, and (3) on Credits.
questions.question: A Tab Group component has three tabs annotated with three separate tab stops. What error has the designer made?

options.text: A Tab Group component gets a single tab stop with arrow key operation
The icons should have their own focus order annotation
A Tab Group component gets a single tab stop with Shift + Tab operation
They used the reading order annotation marker instead of the focus marker

feedback.a: Correct. Following the ARIA tabs pattern, a Tab Group receives a single tab stop with left/right arrow keys to move between tabs.
feedback.b: Incorrect. Icons within tabs are decorative and should not receive separate focus.
feedback.c: Incorrect. Arrow keys, not Shift+Tab, are used to navigate between tabs within a Tab Group.
feedback.d: Incorrect. The error is in the number of tab stops, not the type of annotation marker.
